message:
fcp-graphql: RAII cleanup + byte-keyed dedup (br-wd6ei, br-upp69)

br-flywheel_connectors-wd6ei [HIGH]
  The dedup map in client.rs previously only removed its entry on
  normal completion — a cancelled owner (outer future dropped mid-
  await) or a panicking owner left the entry in place, so every
  subsequent same-body caller got served the cached Shared<>
  indefinitely and the map grew monotonically with unique
  cancelled-request bodies.

  Replace the explicit post-await `remove` with a DedupGuard RAII
  struct whose Drop impl removes the entry unconditionally. The
  common path is a synchronous try_lock+remove; under lock contention
  Drop spawns a short async task so Drop itself stays non-blocking.
  Tests added for both code paths plus an empty-key tolerance test.

br-flywheel_connectors-upp69 [LOW]
  The dedup map was keyed on a 64-bit `DefaultHasher` (SipHash)
  output, so any two request bodies that happen to SipHash to the
  same u64 would merge into one in-flight entry and the second
  caller would be served the response intended for the first.
  Adversarial collisions are attainable because DefaultHasher is
  not HashDoS-resistant.

  Switch to `HashMap<Vec<u8>, SharedRequestFuture>` keyed on the
  raw request-body bytes. Key equality is now byte-for-byte request
  equality, collision-free. Regression test proves two bodies that
  differ in exactly one byte occupy distinct map entries and return
  distinct responses.

Files: crates/fcp-graphql/src/client.rs (+162 -71).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
